Mains Power] --> P2[Prime Mover] P2 -->|P_shaft| P3[Coupling] P3 -->|eta_mech| P4[Impeller] P4 -->|eta_man| P5[Water in Volute] P5 -->|eta_vol effect via Q_act| P6[Delivery Flow] P1 --> P2 P2 --> P3 P3 -.->|Bearing Friction Loss| PL1[Heat Loss] P4 -.->|Friction in Passages| PL2[Eddy and Friction Loss] P5 -.->|Leakage Through Wear Ring| PL3[Recirculation Loss] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagram:** Each block transition represents a multiplicative efficiency step. The cumulative efficiency from the mains to the delivery is $\eta_{overall}$. The losses (dashed arrows) explain why $\eta_{overall}$ can never equal 100 %. ### 4.3 Manometric Head Computation Block ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Pressure Head Term
P_d minus P_s over rho g] --> M[Manometric Head H_m] B[Velocity Head Term
V_d squared minus V_s squared over 2g] --> M C[Static Head Term
Z_d minus Z_s] --> M M --> D[Sum Equals H_m in metres] ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define overall efficiency of a centrifugal pump. List the three sub-efficiencies whose product equals it. **Model Answer:** Overall efficiency $\eta_{overall}$ of a centrifugal pump is the ratio of the useful hydraulic power output (water power) to the mechanical power supplied at the shaft, expressed as a percentage. The three sub-efficiencies are: (i) **Manometric efficiency** — accounts for hydraulic losses in the impeller and casing; (ii) **Mechanical efficiency** — accounts for bearing and gland friction losses; (iii) **Volumetric efficiency** — accounts for leakage losses through the wear-ring clearances. Their product gives the overall efficiency. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Why is the manometric head of a centrifugal pump measured between the suction and delivery flanges and not between the suction reservoir free surface and the delivery reservoir free surface? **Model Answer:** Measuring between the **suction and delivery flanges of the pump** eliminates the inclusion of friction losses in the suction and delivery pipe lines. The manometric head represents only the energy *imparted by the pump* to the water, not the total system head. If measured from reservoir surfaces, the result would include pipe friction losses that the pump does not impart — leading to an erroneously low efficiency calculation. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) #### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** A centrifugal pump test rig yields the following data at a constant speed of 1450 rpm: | Trial | Suction Gauge (bar) | Delivery Gauge (bar) | Static Head (m) | Pipe Diameter (mm) | Discharge (L/s) | Shaft Torque (N·m) | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | –0.18 | 1.20 | 0.30 | 60 | 10 | 9.5 | | 2 | –0.15 | 1.45 | 0.30 | 60 | 8.5 | 10.2 | | 3 | –0.10 | 1.75 | 0.30 | 60 | 6.0 | 11.0 | **Required:** **(a)** Calculate the manometric head for each trial. **(b)** Calculate the overall efficiency for each trial and identify the Best Efficiency Point (BEP). **Part (a) — Manometric Head (7 marks):** Since pipe diameters are equal (60 mm), $V_s = V_d$, so velocity-head difference = 0. **Trial 1:** $$ H_{m1} = \frac{(1.20 - (-0.18)) \times 10^{5}}{9810} + 0.30 $$ $$ H_{m1} = \frac{1.38 \times 10^{5}}{9810} + 0.30 = 14.067 + 0.30 = 14.367\ \text{m} $$ **Trial 2:** $$ H_{m2} = \frac{(1.45 + 0.15) \times 10^{5}}{9810} + 0.30 = \frac{1.60 \times 10^{5}}{9810} + 0.30 $$ $$ H_{m2} = 16.31 + 0.30 = 16.61\ \text{m} $$ **Trial 3:** $$ H_{m3} = \frac{(1.75 + 0.10) \times 10^{5}}{9810} + 0.30 = \frac{1.85 \times 10^{5}}{9810} + 0.30 $$ $$ H_{m3} = 18.86 + 0.30 = 19.16\ \text{m} $$ **Part (b) — Overall Efficiency (7 marks):** Shaft power: $P_{shaft} = \dfrac{2 \pi N T}{60} = \dfrac{2 \pi \times 1450 \times T}{60} = 151.84 \times T\ \text{W}$ **Trial 1:** $P_{shaft1} = 151.84 \times 9.5 = 1442.5\ \text{W}$. $P_{water1} = 9810 \times 0.010 \times 14.367 = 1409.4\ \text{W}$. $$ \eta_1 = \frac{1409.4}{1442.5} = 0.9771 = 97.71\ \% $$ **Trial 2:** $P_{shaft2} = 151.84 \times 10.2 = 1548.8\ \text{W}$. $P_{water2} = 9810 \times 0.0085 \times 16.61 = 1384.9\ \text{W}$. $$ \eta_2 = \frac{1384.9}{1548.8} = 0.8942 = 89.42\ \% $$ **Trial 3:** $P_{shaft3} = 151.84 \times 11.0 = 1670.3\ \text{W}$. $P_{water3} = 9810 \times 0.0060 \times 19.16 = 1127.5\ \text{W}$. $$ \eta_3 = \frac{1127.5}{1670.3} = 0.6750 = 67.50\ \% $$ **Best Efficiency Point (BEP):** Trial 1 has the highest overall efficiency at **97.71 %**; therefore the BEP corresponds to discharge $Q = 10\ \text{L/s}$ at $H_m = 14.37\ \text{m}$. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** In Question A(b), many students forget to convert the discharge from L/s to m³/s. **1 mark is deducted** for unit inconsistency. Always convert $Q$ to m³/s before multiplying with $\rho g$. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Alternative Choice]** **Question B (a) — [7 marks]:** A centrifugal pump delivers water at a rate of 12 L/s against a manometric head of 18 m. The shaft speed is 1500 rpm and the torque measured on the shaft is 14 N·m. Calculate: (i) Shaft power input (ii) Water power output (iii) Overall efficiency **Model Solution:** **Part (i) — Shaft Power:** $$ P_{shaft} = \frac{2 \pi N T}{60} = \frac{2 \pi \times 1500 \times 14}{60} = 2199.11\ \text{W} \approx 2.199\ \text{kW} $$ **Part (ii) — Water Power:** $$ P_{water} = \rho g Q H_m = 1000 \times 9.81 \times (12 \times 10^{-3}) \times 18 = 2118.96\ \text{W} \approx 2.119\ \text{kW} $$ **Part (iii) — Overall Efficiency:** $$ \eta_{overall} = \frac{P_{water}}{P_{shaft}} = \frac{2118.96}{2199.11} = 0.9635 = 96.35\ \% $$ **Question B (b) — [7 marks]:** Explain with a neat sketch the working of a centrifugal pump. How does the volute casing contribute to pressure recovery? **Model Answer:** A centrifugal pump consists of (i) an **impeller** with curved vanes rotating inside a casing; (ii) a **suction pipe** that delivers water to the eye (centre) of the impeller; (iii) a **volute casing** of increasing cross-sectional area; (iv) a **delivery pipe** that discharges the water; and (v) a **shaft** driven by a motor through a coupling. When the impeller rotates, the water at the eye is flung outward by **centrifugal force** along the impeller vanes. The water gains both **kinetic energy** (velocity) and **pressure energy** (centrifugal pressure head). The volute casing collects this high-velocity water in a progressively widening passage (an expanding spiral). As the cross-section enlarges, the velocity of water **decreases** (continuity principle) and, by **Bernoulli's principle**, the pressure rises — this is the pressure-recovery function of the volute. The water finally leaves the casing through the delivery pipe with high static pressure and low velocity. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** In Question B(b), students often confuse the **centrifugal pressure head** ($\omega^2 r^2 / 2g$) with the manometric head. Remember: **centrifugal pressure head** is what the impeller imparts inside the rotating frame, while **manometric head** is the net head measured between flanges in the stationary frame and includes losses. Drawing a labelled diagram with arrows showing water flow direction is mandatory for full marks. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Board Examination** - **Overall efficiency** = Water power / Shaft power — the *ultimate performance metric*. - **Manometric head** is the energy imparted **per unit weight** of water; measured in **metres of water column**. - Apply the energy equation between the **pump suction and delivery flanges** — *not* reservoir surfaces. - $P_{shaft} = \dfrac{2 \pi N T}{60}$ — remember the **60** (not 60²) in the denominator. - $P_{water} = \rho g Q H_m$ — convert $Q$ to **m³/s** before computing. - $1\ \text{HP} = 745.7\ \text{W}$; $1\ \text{kW} = 1000\ \text{W}$. - If pipe diameters are equal, $V_s = V_d$, so the **velocity-head term vanishes** — always state this assumption. - **BEP** is the operating point with maximum $\eta_{overall}$ on the characteristic curve. - **Manometric efficiency** may be computed using $Q_{act}$ (modern KTU convention) or $Q_{th}$ (older convention) — read the question carefully. - **Wear-ring clearance** governs volumetric losses; **bearing condition** governs mechanical losses; **impeller surface roughness** governs manometric losses. - **Friction loss in the suction/delivery pipes is NOT included** in manometric head — this is a deliberate design choice to isolate the pump's intrinsic performance. - Always include the **unit and sign convention** (gauge vs. absolute) when writing pressure values.

topics.title: Determination of Viscosity of Newtonian and Non-Newtonian Fluid by Falling Sphere Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOL408 - FLUID MECHANICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of Viscosity of Newtonian and Non-Newtonian Fluid by Falling Sphere Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Viscosity ($\mu$)** is defined as the internal resistance of a fluid to flow, representing the ratio of shear stress ($\tau$) to the shear rate ($\dot{\gamma}$) within the fluid. The **Falling Sphere Method** (also known as the **Stokes' Law Viscometer** or **Hoppler Viscometer** in its precision instrument form) is a classical experimental technique used to determine the **dynamic viscosity** of both Newtonian and Non-Newtonian fluids by measuring the **terminal settling velocity** of a small sphere of known density and diameter as it falls under gravity through a quiescent column of the test fluid.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, the experiment is rigorously categorized under **Course Outcome CO2**: *Apply principles of fluid statics and dynamics to solve engineering problems using appropriate laboratory techniques.*

A **Newtonian Fluid** is one in which the viscosity remains constant irrespective of the applied shear rate (e.g., water, glycerin, castor oil, air). Conversely, a **Non-Newtonian Fluid** exhibits a viscosity that varies with shear rate, often described by empirical models such as the **Ostwald–de Waele Power Law** model, where apparent viscosity $\mu_a$ is a function of the shear rate.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCPOL408 - Module 2):**
> The experiment aims to experimentally verify **Stokes' Law** for laminar flow conditions, calculate the **terminal velocity** of the falling sphere, and compare the **apparent viscosity** behavior between standard Newtonian fluids and Non-Newtonian polymer/slurry samples.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine dropping a **steel ball bearing** through a tall, transparent measuring cylinder filled with **honey**. Initially, the ball accelerates due to gravity ($g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$). However, almost immediately, the honey exerts a **drag force** upward, opposing the motion. As the ball speeds up, the drag force increases until the **net force becomes zero**. At this precise instant, the ball stops accelerating and descends at a **constant velocity** — this is the **terminal velocity** ($v_t$).

The "stickiness" of the honey (i.e., its viscosity) determines how quickly the ball reaches this terminal velocity. **Thicker** fluids produce **slower** terminal velocities; **thinner** fluids allow **faster** descent. By precisely measuring the time taken for the ball to traverse two marked reference points a known distance apart, and knowing the densities of the sphere and the fluid, we can **back-calculate** the fluid's viscosity using **Stokes' Law**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Intuition:** This is the exact same physics that governs a **parachutist** falling through air, a **rain droplet** falling through the atmosphere, a **sediment particle** settling in a river, or the descent of a **dust particle** in a settling chamber of an industrial dust collector. The medical field also uses this principle in the **Falling Ball Viscometer** for blood plasma analysis.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following constants and standard metrics are **mandatorily** used in the lab calculations:

- **Acceleration due to gravity:** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- **Density of water at 25°C:** $\rho_{water} = 997 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- **Density of steel sphere (typical):** $\rho_s \approx 7800 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- **Density of glass sphere (typical):** $\rho_s \approx 2500 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- **Density of glycerin (25°C):** $\rho_f \approx 1260 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- **Standard unit of dynamic viscosity:** $\text{Pascal-second (Pa·s)}$ or equivalently $\text{kg/(m·s)}$
- **CGS unit (often in textbooks):** **Poise (P)**, where $1 \text{ Pa·s} = 10 \text{ P} = 1000 \text{ cP}$

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Tip:** KTU valuation scripts often contain conversion errors. Always remember: $1 \text{ cP} = 10^{-3} \text{ Pa·s}$. The viscosity of water at **20°C is exactly 1.002 cP**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — The Underlying Physics

When a rigid sphere falls under gravity through a viscous fluid, **three forces** act upon it in the vertical direction:

1. **Gravitational Pull (Weight, $F_g$)** — directed downward:
   $$F_g = m_s \cdot g = \frac{4}{3} \pi r^3 \rho_s \cdot g$$

2. **Buoyancy Force ($F_b$)** — directed upward (Archimedes' Principle):
   $$F_b = \frac{4}{3} \pi r^3 \rho_f \cdot g$$

3. **Viscous Drag Force ($F_d$)** — directed upward, opposing motion. For **laminar flow** (Reynolds number $\ll 1$), this is given by **Stokes' Law (1851)**:
   $$F_d = 6 \pi \mu r v$$

where:
- $r$ = radius of the sphere (m)
- $\rho_s$ = density of the sphere (kg/m³)
- $\rho_f$ = density of the fluid (kg/m³)
- $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity of the fluid (Pa·s)
- $v$ = instantaneous velocity of the sphere (m/s)

### 2.2 Concept of Terminal Velocity

At the moment the **net force** on the sphere becomes zero, the sphere attains a **constant terminal velocity** $v_t$:

$$F_g = F_b + F_d$$

Substituting the three force equations:

$$\frac{4}{3} \pi r^3 \rho_s g = \frac{4}{3} \pi r^3 \rho_f g + 6 \pi \mu r v_t$$

Rearranging and solving for the **dynamic viscosity** $\mu$:

$$\boxed{\mu = \frac{2 \cdot r^2 \cdot g \cdot (\rho_s - \rho_f)}{9 \cdot v_t}}$$

The **terminal velocity** is determined experimentally as:

$$v_t = \frac{L}{t}$$

where:
- $L$ = distance between the two reference marks on the measuring cylinder (m)
- $t$ = time taken for the sphere to traverse the distance $L$ (s)

### 2.3 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

To ensure clarity for KTU lab report writing and viva voce, the logical sequence of the experiment is as follows:

- **Step 1 — Setup Validation:** Verify that the fluid column is sufficiently tall and quiescent (no air bubbles, no thermal convection currents). The sphere must fall along the **central axis** to avoid wall effects.
- **Step 2 — Laminar Flow Check:** Calculate the **Reynolds number** for the falling sphere: $Re = \dfrac{\rho_f \cdot v_t \cdot d}{\mu}$. Stokes' Law is strictly valid only when $Re < 1$ (ideally $Re < 0.1$).
- **Step 3 — Sphere Selection:** Choose a sphere diameter such that terminal velocity is low enough to maintain laminar flow but high enough for accurate timing.
- **Step 4 — Timing Zone:** Discard the **entry zone** (where the sphere is still accelerating) and the **exit zone** (where the sphere may experience bottom effects). The timing is recorded between two **reference marks** separated by a known distance $L$.
- **Step 5 — Repetition:** Repeat the experiment at least **3 to 5 times** with different sphere radii to verify consistency and average the viscosity values.
- **Step 6 — Non-Newtonian Extension:** For Non-Newtonian fluids (e.g., CMC solutions, shear-thinning polymer solutions), the apparent viscosity is calculated and plotted against the calculated shear rate.

### 2.4 Non-Newtonian Fluid Analysis (Power-Law Model)

For a **Non-Newtonian fluid** that obeys the **Ostwald–de Waele Power Law**:

$$\tau = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^n$$

where:
- $K$ = consistency index (Pa·s$^n$)
- $n$ = flow behavior index (dimensionless)
- $n < 1$: **Pseudoplastic (shear-thinning)**
- $n > 1$: **Dilatant (shear-thickening)**
- $n = 1$: **Newtonian** ($K$ becomes $\mu$)

The **apparent viscosity** is defined as:

$$\mu_a = \frac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}} = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$$

For a sphere falling in a power-law fluid, the terminal velocity is given by a modified equation (e.g., **Brinkman or Chhabra–Seychelles equation**), but in the KTU lab context, the **average shear rate** at the sphere surface is approximated as:

$$\dot{\gamma} \approx \frac{v_t}{d}$$

where $d$ is the sphere diameter. Using multiple sphere diameters and plotting $\ln(\mu_a)$ vs. $\ln(\dot{\gamma})$, the **flow behavior index $n$** and **consistency index $K$** can be obtained from the slope and intercept respectively.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Units |
|---|--------------------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $F_d = 6 \pi \mu r v$ | Stokes' Drag Force | N |
| 2 | $F_g = \frac{4}{3} \pi r^3 \rho_s g$ | Gravitational Weight of Sphere | N |
| 3 | $F_b = \frac{4}{3} \pi r^3 \rho_f g$ | Buoyant Force on Sphere | N |
| 4 | $\mu = \dfrac{2 r^2 g (\rho_s - \rho_f)}{9 v_t}$ | Dynamic Viscosity (Newtonian) | Pa·s |
| 5 | $v_t = L / t$ | Terminal Velocity | m/s |
| 6 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho_f v_t d}{\mu}$ | Reynolds Number Check (must be $\ll 1$) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\mu_a = K \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$ | Apparent Viscosity (Power Law) | Pa·s |
| 8 | $\dot{\gamma} \approx v_t / d$ | Average Shear Rate at Sphere Surface | s$^{-1}$ |
| 9 | $\tau = K \dot{\gamma}^n$ | Power-Law Shear Stress | Pa |
| 10 | $d = 2r$ | Diameter–Radius Relationship | m |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Unit Conversion Reminder:** 
> - Viscosity: $1 \text{ Pa·s} = 10 \text{ Poise} = 1000 \text{ cP}$
> - Length: $1 \text{ mm} = 10^{-3} \text{ m}$
> - Time: $1 \text{ minute} = 60 \text{ s}$

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The falling sphere viscometer, despite being a classical method, is extensively used in:

- **Petroleum Industry:** Viscosity grading of crude oil, drilling muds, and heavy fuel oils.
- **Pharmaceutical Industry:** Quality control of syrups, ointments, and parenteral fluids.
- **Polymer Processing:** Characterization of polymer melts and solutions in injection molding design.
- **Food Industry:** Viscosity measurement of chocolate, ketchup, honey, and dairy products.
- **Geotechnical Engineering:** Settling velocity of soil particles in sedimentation tanks and slurry transport pipelines.
- **Cement and Paint Industry:** Quality assurance of cement slurries, paints, and coatings.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Working Viscosity Formula

Starting from the **force balance at terminal velocity** (no acceleration, net force = 0):

$$F_{net} = F_g - F_b - F_d = 0$$

$$F_g = F_b + F_d$$

**Step 1: Substitute the expressions for each force.**

$$\frac{4}{3} \pi r^3 \rho_s g = \frac{4}{3} \pi r^3 \rho_f g + 6 \pi \mu r v_t$$

**Step 2: Bring the buoyancy term to the left-hand side.**

$$\frac{4}{3} \pi r^3 \rho_s g - \frac{4}{3} \pi r^3 \rho_f g = 6 \pi \mu r v_t$$

**Step 3: Factor out the common term $\frac{4}{3} \pi r^3 g$.**

$$\frac{4}{3} \pi r^3 g (\rho_s - \rho_f) = 6 \pi \mu r v_t$$

**Step 4: Divide both sides by $6 \pi r$.**

$$\frac{4}{3} \pi r^3 g (\rho_s - \rho_f) \cdot \frac{1}{6 \pi r} = \mu v_t$$

**Step 5: Simplify the left-hand side.**

$$\frac{4 r^2 g (\rho_s - \rho_f)}{18} = \mu v_t$$

$$\frac{2 r^2 g (\rho_s - \rho_f)}{9} = \mu v_t$$

**Step 6: Solve explicitly for $\mu$ (the final working formula).**

$$\boxed{\mu = \frac{2 r^2 g (\rho_s - \rho_f)}{9 v_t}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Derivation Logic Note:** The $r^2$ dependence shows that **doubling the radius** of the sphere increases the terminal velocity by a factor of **4**, which is why small spheres are preferred for high-viscosity fluids to keep velocities measurable in the lab.

### 3.2 Inclusion of Wall and End Corrections (Advanced Refinement)

In a real KTU lab experiment, the cylinder is **not infinitely wide**, and the sphere travels a **finite distance**. Therefore, two correction factors are applied for greater accuracy:

**Wall Correction Factor** (by **Faxén, 1923**):

$$\mu_{corrected} = \mu_{measured} \cdot \left[ 1 - 2.104 \left(\frac{d}{D}\right) + 2.09 \left(\frac{d}{D}\right)^3 - 0.95 \left(\frac{d}{D}\right)^5 \right]^{-1}$$

where $D$ is the cylinder diameter and $d$ is the sphere diameter.

**End Correction (Finite Length Effect)** — generally negligible if the timing zone $L$ is greater than **5 times the cylinder diameter $D$**.

### 3.3 Detailed Numerical Worked Example (Model Lab Calculation)

**Problem Statement (KTU Typical Lab Problem):**

A steel sphere of diameter $d = 2 \text{ mm}$ falls through a column of glycerin. The distance between the two reference marks is $L = 200 \text{ mm}$. The average time recorded is $t = 4.85 \text{ s}$. Given $\rho_s = 7800 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\rho_f = 1260 \text{ kg/m}^3$, and $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$, calculate the dynamic viscosity of glycerin and verify Stokes' Law validity.

**Step 1: Convert all dimensions to SI units (meters).**

$$d = 2 \text{ mm} = 2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$$

$$r = \frac{d}{2} = 1 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 10^{-3} \text{ m}$$

$$L = 200 \text{ mm} = 0.200 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2: Calculate the terminal velocity.**

$$v_t = \frac{L}{t} = \frac{0.200 \text{ m}}{4.85 \text{ s}} = 0.04124 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3: Substitute into the viscosity formula.**

$$\mu = \frac{2 \cdot r^2 \cdot g \cdot (\rho_s - \rho_f)}{9 \cdot v_t}$$

$$\mu = \frac{2 \cdot (10^{-3})^2 \cdot 9.81 \cdot (7800 - 1260)}{9 \cdot 0.04124}$$

**Step 4: Evaluate the numerator step-by-step.**

$$\text{Numerator} = 2 \cdot 10^{-6} \cdot 9.81 \cdot 6540$$

$$= 2 \cdot 10^{-6} \cdot 64163.4$$

$$= 0.128327 \text{ N·m/s}$$

**Step 5: Evaluate the denominator.**

$$\text{Denominator} = 9 \cdot 0.04124 = 0.37116 \text{ (m/s) per unit (N·s/m²)}$$

**Step 6: Divide to obtain viscosity.**

$$\mu = \frac{0.128327}{0.37116} = 0.3458 \text{ Pa·s}$$

**Step 7: Convert to centipoise for comparison with literature.**

$$\mu = 0.3458 \text{ Pa·s} \times 1000 = 345.8 \text{ cP}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Literature Comparison:** The standard viscosity of glycerin at **25°C is approximately 954 cP**, and at **20°C is around 1500 cP**. The discrepancy indicates that our experimental value is lower, primarily due to **temperature variations**, **wall effects**, and **timing errors**. This is fully expected in a student lab experiment.

**Step 8: Verify Stokes' Law validity using the Reynolds number.**

$$Re = \frac{\rho_f \cdot v_t \cdot d}{\mu} = \frac{1260 \cdot 0.04124 \cdot 0.002}{0.3458}$$

$$Re = \frac{0.10392}{0.3458} = 0.3005$$

Since $Re = 0.30 < 1$, **Stokes' Law is valid** for this experiment. ✓

### 3.4 Non-Newtonian Worked Example (Power-Law Fluid)

**Problem:** A polymer solution exhibits Non-Newtonian behavior. Two spheres of diameters $d_1 = 2 \text{ mm}$ and $d_2 = 3 \text{ mm}$ are dropped. The corresponding terminal velocities are $v_1 = 0.025 \text{ m/s}$ and $v_2 = 0.055 \text{ m/s}$. Estimate the flow behavior index $n$.

**Step 1: Compute apparent viscosity for each sphere using the Newtonian formula (as an approximation for low $Re$):**

$$\mu_{a1} = \frac{2 r_1^2 g (\rho_s - \rho_f)}{9 v_1}, \quad \mu_{a2} = \frac{2 r_2^2 g (\rho_s - \rho_f)}{9 v_2}$$

**Step 2: Compute the average shear rate for each sphere:**

$$\dot{\gamma}_1 = \frac{v_1}{d_1} = \frac{0.025}{0.002} = 12.5 \text{ s}^{-1}$$

$$\dot{\gamma}_2 = \frac{v_2}{d_2} = \frac{0.055}{0.003} = 18.33 \text{ s}^{-1}$$

**Step 3: Use the power-law relationship to estimate $n$:**

$$\frac{\mu_{a1}}{\mu_{a2}} = \left(\frac{\dot{\gamma}_1}{\dot{\gamma}_2}\right)^{n-1}$$

$$\ln\left(\frac{\mu_{a1}}{\mu_{a2}}\right) = (n-1) \cdot \ln\left(\frac{\dot{\gamma}_1}{\dot{\gamma}_2}\right)$$

$$n = 1 + \frac{\ln(\mu_{a1} / \mu_{a2})}{\ln(\dot{\gamma}_1 / \dot{\gamma}_2)}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Practical Insight:** If $n < 1$, the fluid is **pseudoplastic** (shear-thinning), common in polymer solutions and paints. If $n > 1$, it is **dilatant** (shear-thickening), seen in concentrated starch suspensions. This is a frequently asked viva question.

### 3.5 Analytical Error Matrix (Common Sources of Experimental Error)

| # | Source of Error | Effect on $\mu$ | Mitigation Strategy |
|---|----------------|-----------------|---------------------|
| 1 | Sphere not centered in cylinder | Measured $v_t$ lower → $\mu$ appears higher | Use guide ring or plumb line at top |
| 2 | Air bubbles adhering to sphere | Effective $\rho_s$ lower → $\mu$ under-predicted | Wet the sphere before insertion |
| 3 | Temperature variation | $\mu$ highly temperature-dependent | Use thermostat bath, record $T$ |
| 4 | Sphere not at terminal velocity in timing zone | $v_t$ inaccurate | Discard entry/exit zones, use longer $L$ |
| 5 | Wall effects (cylinder too narrow) | $v_t$ lower → $\mu$ higher | Maintain $d/D < 0.1$ |
| 6 | Inaccurate timing (human reaction time) | Random error in $t$ | Use electronic timer / photogates |
| 7 | Sphere density uncertainty | Systematic error in $(\rho_s - \rho_f)$ | Calibrate sphere mass on balance |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied

All Mermaid diagrams in this section use:
- **Purely alphanumeric node IDs** prefixed with letters (e.g., `stepA`, `node1`)
- **No reserved keywords** (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`) used as standalone node names
- **No markdown formatting** (`**`, `*`, or HTML) inside double-quoted labels
- **Raw uppercase alphanumeric text** for clarity

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Falling Sphere Viscometer

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[FALLING SPHERE VISCOMETER SYSTEM] --> B1[FLUID COLUMN ASSEMBLY]
    A1 --> B2[MEASUREMENT SUBSYSTEM]
    A1 --> B3[DATA ACQUISITION UNIT]
    A1 --> B4[CONTROL ENVIRONMENT]
    B1 --> C1[GRADUATED GLASS CYLINDER]
    B1 --> C2[THERMOSTATIC JACKET]
    B1 --> C3[SPHERE RELEASE MECHANISM]
    B1 --> C4[CALIBRATED REFERENCE MARKS TOP AND BOTTOM]
    B2 --> D1[STEEL OR GLASS SPHERE]
    B2 --> D2[STOPWATCH OR PHOTOGATE TIMER]
    B2 --> D3[VERNIER CALIPER FOR DIAMETER]
    B2 --> D4[ANALYTICAL BALANCE FOR MASS]
    B3 --> E1[OBSERVED FALL TIME T]
    B3 --> E2[CALCULATED TERMINAL VELOCITY V]
    B3 --> E3[COMPUTED VISCOSITY MU]
    B3 --> E4[RHEOLOGICAL PLOT FOR NON NEWTONIAN]
    B4 --> F1[TEMPERATURE SENSOR]
    B4 --> F2[ANTI VIBRATION TABLE]
    B4 --> F3[STANDARD DENSITY FLUIDS]
```

### 4.3 Experimental Procedure Flowchart (Step-by-Step Logic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[START EXPERIMENT] --> P2[FILL CYLINDER WITH TEST FLUID]
    P2 --> P3[ENSURE FLUID IS QUIESCENT AND DEGASSED]
    P3 --> P4[MEASURE FLUID DENSITY RHOF USING PYCNOMETER]
    P4 --> P5[SELECT SPHERE OF KNOWN DIAMETER D AND DENSITY RHOS]
    P5 --> P6[WET SPHERE AND RELEASE GENTLY AT CENTER OF CYLINDER TOP]
    P6 --> P7[START TIMER AT FIRST REFERENCE MARK]
    P7 --> P8[STOP TIMER AT SECOND REFERENCE MARK]
    P8 --> P9[RECORD FALL TIME T]
    P9 --> P10[CALCULATE TERMINAL VELOCITY V EQUALS L BY T]
    P10 --> P11[COMPUTE VISCOSITY MU USING STOKES FORMULA]
    P11 --> P12[CALCULATE REYNOLDS NUMBER RE]
    P12 --> P13{IS RE LESS THAN ONE}
    P13 -->|YES| P14[RECORD VALID RESULT]
    P13 -->|NO| P15[SELECT SMALLER SPHERE AND REPEAT]
    P14 --> P16[REPEAT 3 TO 5 TIMES FOR AVERAGING]
    P16 --> P17[FOR NON NEWTONIAN PLOT MUAPPARENT VS SHEAR RATE]
    P17 --> P18[END EXPERIMENT]
```

### 4.4 Force Balance Diagram (Text-Based Schematic)

```
            SPHERE FALLING IN VISCOUS FLUID
            ==================================

                          ↑ F_b  (Buoyancy)
                          |
                          |
                  _______ ↓ _______
                 /                \
                |     SPHERE       |  →  v_t (downward)
                |       ●          |
                 \________________/
                          |
                          |
                          ↓ F_g  (Weight, downward)
                          ↑
                          | F_d  (Viscous Drag, upward)

    EQUILIBRIUM CONDITION AT TERMINAL VELOCITY:
    F_g  =  F_b  +  F_d
    (4/3)πr³ρ_s·g  =  (4/3)πr³ρ_f·g  +  6πμr·v_t
```

### 4.5 Comparison Logic Flow: Newtonian vs. Non-Newtonian Behavior

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    N1[OBSERVE SPHERE FALL] --> N2{IS FLUID NEWTONIAN}
    N2 -->|YES| N3[CONSTANT VISCOSITY REGARDLESS OF SHEAR]
    N2 -->|NO| N4[MEASURE APPARENT VISCOSITY FOR EACH SPHERE SIZE]
    N3 --> N5[PLOT MU VERSUS R TO VERIFY STOKES LAW]
    N4 --> N6[PLOT LOG MUAPPARENT VERSUS LOG SHEARRATE]
    N6 --> N7[SLOPE GIVES N MINUS ONE]
    N6 --> N8[INTERCEPT GIVES LOG K]
    N5 --> N9[VALIDATE LAMINAR FLOW RE LESS THAN ONE]
    N7 --> N10[CLASSIFY PSEUDOPLASTIC OR DILATANT]
    N8 --> N10
    N9 --> N11[REPORT FINAL VISCOSITY AND RHEOLOGICAL PARAMETERS]
    N10 --> N11
```
