# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the terms: (i) Vena Contracta, (ii) Coefficient of Contraction, (iii) Coefficient of Discharge. State the relationship between them.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Vena Contracta:** The section of the jet, just downstream of the orifice, where the cross-sectional area of the jet is minimum and the streamlines are parallel, is called the **vena contracta**. It is located at approximately **$0.5d$** from the orifice plane for a sharp-edged circular orifice.
>
> **Coefficient of Contraction ($C_c$):** It is the ratio of the area of the jet at the vena contracta ($a_c$) to the area of the orifice ($a$).
> $$C_c = \frac{a_c}{a}$$
>
> **Coefficient of Discharge ($C_d$):** It is the ratio of the actual discharge through the orifice to the theoretical discharge.
> $$C_d = \frac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{theoretical}}$$
>
> **Relationship:** $C_d = C_c \times C_v$

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State Torricelli's theorem. Under what conditions is the theoretical velocity at the orifice equal to $\sqrt{2gH}$?**

**Model Answer:**

> **Torricelli's Theorem:** The theoretical velocity of a fluid jet issuing from a small orifice under a constant head $H$ is given by $V_t = \sqrt{2gH}$.
>
> **Conditions for validity:**
> 1. The orifice is small compared to the head ($d \ll H$).
> 2. The tank cross-section is much larger than the orifice area (so $V_1 \approx 0$).
> 3. The fluid is ideal (inviscid, incompressible) — no friction losses.
> 4. The pressure at the free surface and at the jet exit is atmospheric (gauge = 0).
> 5. Steady-state flow with constant head.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Module Internal Choice:** Answer **ONE** of the following. Each carries 14 marks, split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Derive an expression for the theoretical velocity of efflux through a small orifice using Bernoulli's equation. State clearly the assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A sharp-edged circular orifice of diameter **$10 \text{ mm}$** is fitted to a vertical tank under a constant head of **$50 \text{ cm}$**. The jet strikes a horizontal surface at a horizontal distance of **$80 \text{ cm}$** and vertical drop of **$40 \text{ cm}$** from the vena contracta. The measured jet diameter at vena contracta is **$8.0 \text{ mm}$**. In **25 seconds**, a volume of **$2.95 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3$** is collected. Calculate $C_v$, $C_c$, and $C_d$. Verify the relation $C_d = C_c \times C_v$. Take $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a):

Apply Bernoulli's equation between point 1 (free surface) and point 2 (orifice exit):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{p_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^{2}}{2g} + z_1 &= \frac{p_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^{2}}{2g} + z_2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Assumptions:**
1. Fluid is ideal (no viscous loss).
2. Flow is steady, incompressible.
3. $p_1 = p_2 = p_{atm}$ → gauge head terms vanish.
4. $V_1 \approx 0$ (large tank area).
5. $z_1 - z_2 = H$ (head above orifice).

$$
\begin{aligned}
0 + 0 + H &= 0 + \frac{V_t^{2}}{2g} + 0 \\
\therefore V_t &= \sqrt{2gH} \quad \text{(Torricelli's velocity)}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Model Solution (b):

**Step 1: Theoretical velocity**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_t &= \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.50} = \sqrt{9.81} = 3.132 \text{ m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Actual velocity (trajectory method)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_a &= x \sqrt{\frac{g}{2y}} = 0.80 \times \sqrt{\frac{9.81}{2 \times 0.40}} \\
&= 0.80 \times \sqrt{12.2625} = 0.80 \times 3.502 = 2.801 \text{ m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Coefficient of Velocity**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_v &= \frac{V_a}{V_t} = \frac{2.801}{3.132} = 0.894
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Note (2024 Pattern):** $C_v = 0.894$ is below the typical 0.95–0.99 range, indicating either short orifice, edge imperfection, or a partially submerged setup. **Students should comment on this in 1–2 lines to secure the 'interpretation' sub-mark.**

**Step 4: Coefficient of Contraction**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_c &= \left(\frac{d_c}{d}\right)^{2} = \left(\frac{8.0}{10.0}\right)^{2} = (0.80)^{2} = 0.640
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Actual discharge**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{actual} &= \frac{V}{t} = \frac{2.95 \times 10^{-3}}{25} = 1.18 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Orifice area**

$$
\begin{aligned}
a &= \frac{\pi}{4}(0.010)^{2} = 7.854 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7: Theoretical discharge**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_t &= a \cdot V_t = 7.854 \times 10^{-5} \times 3.132 = 2.460 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8: Coefficient of Discharge**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_d &= \frac{Q_{actual}}{Q_t} = \frac{1.18 \times 10^{-4}}{2.460 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.4797
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 9: Verification**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_c \times C_v &= 0.640 \times 0.894 = 0.572
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vert C_d - C_c \cdot C_v \vert &= \vert 0.4797 - 0.572 \vert = 0.092
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Comment on deviation:** The discrepancy (0.092) exceeds 5%, suggesting measurement error in the volume-collected reading (likely a partial jet loss or parallax in the piezometer). In board terms, **2 marks are reserved for stating this fact and attributing it to a probable source.**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]`

**(a)** What is vena contracta? Explain with a neat sketch how the three coefficients $C_c$, $C_v$, and $C_d$ are determined experimentally for a sharp-edged orifice. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In an orifice experiment, the head of water above the orifice is **$40 \text{ cm}$**. The orifice diameter is **$12 \text{ mm}$** and the jet diameter at vena contracta is **$9.5 \text{ mm}$**. The jet is found to fall at a horizontal distance of **$75 \text{ cm}$** and a vertical drop of **$38 \text{ cm}$** from the vena contracta. If **$3.6 \text{ litres}$** of water are collected in **$20 \text{ seconds}$**, find all three coefficients and verify the relation $C_d = C_c \times C_v$. Take $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a):

**Vena Contracta:** It is the section of minimum cross-sectional area of a fluid jet, located just downstream of a sharp-edged orifice where the converging streamlines become parallel. For a sharp-edged circular orifice, it occurs at a distance of approximately **$0.5d$** from the orifice plane.

**Experimental determination:**

- **$C_c$:** Measure jet diameter $d_c$ at vena contracta using a traveling microscope or vernier hook gauge. Then $C_c = (d_c/d)^2$.
- **$C_v$:** Trajectory method — measure $x$ and $y$ of the jet's parabolic path, then $C_v = x / (2\sqrt{yH})$.
- **$C_d$:** Volume-time method — collect discharge for time $t$, find volume $V$, compute $Q_{actual} = V/t$, then $C_d = Q_{actual} / (a \sqrt{2gH})$.

**Schematic (refer to Section 4.2 Mermaid diagram):**
The tank holds water, the orifice is fitted on the vertical face, the vena contracta forms downstream, the jet follows a parabolic trajectory, and a collecting tank catches the water.

#### Model Solution (b):

**Step 1: Theoretical velocity**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_t &= \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.40} = \sqrt{7.848} = 2.801 \text{ m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Actual velocity**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_a &= x \sqrt{\frac{g}{2y}} = 0.75 \times \sqrt{\frac{9.81}{2 \times 0.38}} \\
&= 0.75 \times \sqrt{12.908} = 0.75 \times 3.593 = 2.695 \text{ m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: $C_v$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_v &= \frac{V_a}{V_t} = \frac{2.695}{2.801} = 0.962
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: $C_c$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_c &= \left(\frac{9.5}{12.0}\right)^{2} = (0.7917)^{2} = 0.6267
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Orifice area**

$$
\begin{aligned}
a &= \frac{\pi}{4}(0.012)^{2} = 1.131 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Actual discharge**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{actual} &= \frac{3.6 \times 10^{-3}}{20} = 1.80 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7: Theoretical discharge**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_t &= a \cdot V_t = 1.131 \times 10^{-4} \times 2.801 = 3.168 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8: $C_d$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_d &= \frac{Q_{actual}}{Q_t} = \frac{1.80 \times 10^{-4}}{3.168 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.568
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 9: Verification**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_c \times C_v &= 0.6267 \times 0.962 = 0.6029
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Deviation} &= \vert 0.568 - 0.6029 \vert = 0.035 \;\; (\approx 5.8\%)
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Comment on deviation:** The 5.8% deviation is within the **acceptable experimental tolerance of 5–10%** for a KTU undergraduate lab. The likely source of error is slight splashing loss during collection, parallax in measuring the jet diameter $d_c$, or fluctuating head $H$ in the supply tank.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in the trajectory formula.** Always write $C_v = x / (2\sqrt{yH})$, NOT $C_v = x / \sqrt{yH}$. The 2 comes from $\sqrt{2y} \cdot \sqrt{2H} = \sqrt{4yH} = 2\sqrt{yH}$. *Loss: up to 3 marks.*
> 2. **Not converting units to SI.** $H$, $x$, $y$ in cm and $d$ in mm must all be in **metres** before substitution. *Loss: 2 marks for each wrong unit.*
> 3. **Omitting the verification step.** The line "$C_d = C_c \times C_v$ verified" carries **2 marks by itself** in KTU 2024 scheme.
> 4. **Not commenting on experimental error.** A bare numerical answer with no discussion of the deviation between methods loses the "interpretation" marks.
> 5. **Confusing orifice with mouthpiece.** A mouthpiece has $C_d \approx 0.85$; an orifice has $C_d \approx 0.60$. Writing the wrong range in viva loses marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Read Before Every KTU Exam**

- **Orifice:** Small opening; size << head. Sharp edges essential.
- **Open (free) orifice:** Discharges to atmosphere. Both upstream and downstream sides at $p_{atm}$.
- **Vena contracta:** Minimum jet cross-section, located $\approx 0.5d$ downstream of orifice.
- **$C_c$ (Contraction):** $C_c = a_c / a = (d_c / d)^2$. Typical value: **$0.61$ to $0.65$**.
- **$C_v$ (Velocity):** $C_v = V_a / V_t = x / (2\sqrt{yH})$. Typical value: **$0.95$ to $0.99$**.
- **$C_d$ (Discharge):** $C_d = Q_a / Q_t = V / (t \cdot a \cdot \sqrt{2gH})$. Typical value: **$0.60$ to $0.64$**.
- **Master identity (KILLER formula):** $\boxed{C_d = C_c \times C_v}$ — memorize cold.
- **Torricelli's law:** $V_t = \sqrt{2gH}$ — derivation from Bernoulli, with five assumptions.
- **Trajectory method** for $C_v$ relies on the jet's parabolic path; never use a falling-head approximation in this experiment (head is constant).
- **Acceptable error margin** for KTU labs: **5% to 10%** between $C_d$ (volume method) and $C_d = C_c \times C_v$ (component product).
- **SI unit rule:** Convert cm → m, mm → m, minutes → seconds, litres → m³ **before any substitution**.
- **Top three pitfalls:** (1) Forgetting the factor of 2 in $C_v$. (2) Mixing units. (3) Skipping the verification step.
- **Real-world link:** Industrial flow meters, spillways, IV drip regulators, fire nozzles, and rocket injector plates — all governed by $C_d$ values derived from this exact experiment.
- **Reading order on the answer sheet (KTU 2024 convention):** (i) Given data → (ii) Formulae cited → (iii) Step-by-step substitution with units → (iv) Final boxed answer → (v) Verification + error comment.
